meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Chapter 25 contains the eschatological banquet (vv. 6-8) and the swallowing up of death — one of the most theologically significant passages in Isaiah. Paul quotes verse 8 in 1 Corinthians 15:54. The 12 verses contain mostly orthographic variants with one theologically significant passage.
preamble.notable_variants: Verse 8 — 'He will swallow up death forever' — is the centerpiece. 1QIsaiah-a preserves this reading identically, confirming the antiquity of the resurrection/death-defeat tradition. Verse 6 has a minor variant in the banquet description.

variant_notes: No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
Identical consonantal text. God as a stronghold for the poor and needy is described identically. No impact on meaning.

1QIsaiah-a writes שמנים identically in consonantal form. The eschatological banquet — 'a feast of rich foods, a feast of aged wines' — is preserved in both traditions. The doubled description (shemanim/shemarim, rich foods/aged wines) emphasizes abundance. This is the foundational text for the messianic banquet tradition in Judaism and Christianity.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

verses.variant_rendering: He will swallow up death forever
verses.mt_rendering: He will swallow up death forever
variant_notes: This verse is one of the most theologically consequential in the Hebrew Bible. 1QIsaiah-a reads בלע המות לנצח — identical to the MT: 'He will swallow up death forever (la-netsach).' The verb billa' ('swallow up') reverses the image of death as a devouring monster — now death itself is devoured. Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 15:54 as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection. The continuation — 'and the Lord GOD will wipe away tears from all faces' — is also identical in both traditions. The DSS confirm that this death-defeat theology was present in the pre-Christian text, not a later interpolation.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
Identical consonantal text. The 'hand of the LORD will rest on this mountain' — Zion as the place of divine presence — is preserved identically. No impact on meaning.
